name: VPC Fundamentals
description: Core VPC concepts including subnets, route tables, internet gateways, and NAT gateways
key_facts: VPC spans all AZs in a region
Subnets are AZ-specific
Default VPC has an internet gateway and public subnets
CIDR block range: /16 to /28
common_misconceptions: VPCs span multiple regions (they don't)
Subnets can span AZs (they can't)

name: VPC Peering
description: Non-transitive networking connection between two VPCs
key_facts: Non-transitive: if A peers with B and B peers with C, A cannot reach C
Cannot have overlapping CIDR blocks
Cross-region peering supported but with bandwidth considerations
Security groups can reference peered VPC security groups (same region)
common_misconceptions: VPC peering is transitive (it is not)
Peering works with overlapping CIDRs (it does not)
Peering automatically updates route tables (manual route entry required)

name: Transit Gateway
description: Hub-and-spoke network transit hub for connecting VPCs and on-premises networks
key_facts: Supports hub-and-spoke topology at regional level
Inter-region TGW peering for cross-region connectivity
Supports encryption in transit natively
Centralized monitoring via TGW flow logs and Network Manager
Scales to thousands of VPCs
common_misconceptions: Transit Gateway is global (it's regional, requires peering for cross-region)
TGW peering is transitive (it's not, similar to VPC peering)

name: Direct Connect
description: Dedicated network connection from on-premises to AWS
key_facts: Provides consistent network performance vs internet
Supports 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps dedicated connections
Sub-1Gbps via Direct Connect partners
Public VIF for AWS public services, Private VIF for VPCs
Direct Connect Gateway for multi-region access
common_misconceptions: Direct Connect is encrypted by default (it is NOT encrypted)
Direct Connect provides instant setup (lead time is weeks to months)

name: Site-to-Site VPN
description: IPsec VPN connection between on-premises and AWS over the internet
key_facts: Encrypted by default (IPsec)
Quick to set up compared to Direct Connect
Limited to ~1.25 Gbps per VPN tunnel
Two tunnels for high availability
Can be used as backup for Direct Connect
common_misconceptions: VPN provides consistent latency (it goes over the internet)
Single VPN tunnel is sufficient for HA (you need both tunnels)

name: PrivateLink
description: Private connectivity to services across VPCs without VPC peering
key_facts: Exposes a specific service (not full VPC) to another VPC
Uses ENI in the consumer VPC
Traffic stays on AWS network
Works across accounts and regions
No CIDR overlap concerns
common_misconceptions: PrivateLink provides full VPC-to-VPC connectivity (it's service-level only)
PrivateLink replaces VPC peering (different use cases)

name: Security Groups
description: Stateful firewall at the instance level
key_facts: Stateful: return traffic automatically allowed
Allow rules only (no deny rules)
Can reference other security groups
Evaluated as a whole (all rules combined)
Applied at the ENI level
common_misconceptions: Security groups have deny rules (they only have allow rules)
Security groups are stateless (NACLs are stateless, SGs are stateful)

name: Network ACLs
description: Stateless firewall at the subnet level
key_facts: Stateless: must explicitly allow return traffic
Support both allow and deny rules
Rules evaluated in number order
Applied at the subnet level
Default NACL allows all traffic
common_misconceptions: NACLs are stateful (they are stateless)
All rules are evaluated (rules stop at first match)

name: AWS Organizations
description: Centralized management of multiple AWS accounts
key_facts: Organizational Units (OUs) for hierarchical account grouping
Service Control Policies (SCPs) for guardrails
Consolidated billing across all accounts
Cross-account access via IAM roles
Supports up to 5 levels of OU nesting
common_misconceptions: SCPs grant permissions (they only restrict, they are permission boundaries)
SCPs apply to the management account (they don't)

name: AWS Control Tower
description: Automated multi-account governance with guardrails
key_facts: Landing zone: pre-configured multi-account environment
Guardrails: preventive (SCPs) and detective (Config rules)
Account Factory for standardized account provisioning
Built on Organizations, CloudFormation, Config, SSO
Dashboard for compliance visibility
common_misconceptions: Control Tower replaces Organizations (it builds on top of it)
Guardrails are all preventive (some are detective)

name: IAM Advanced Policies
description: Complex IAM policy evaluation, cross-account access, and federation
key_facts: Policy evaluation: explicit deny > explicit allow > implicit deny
Permission boundaries limit maximum permissions
Cross-account access via IAM roles with trust policies
Session policies further restrict assumed role permissions
Resource-based policies vs identity-based policies
common_misconceptions: Permission boundaries grant permissions (they only limit)
All policies are evaluated independently (they interact)

name: AWS Lambda
description: Serverless compute for event-driven architectures
key_facts: Max execution time: 15 minutes
Max memory: 10 GB
Concurrency limits: 1000 default per region
Cold start latency for VPC-attached Lambdas
Provisioned concurrency for predictable performance
common_misconceptions: Lambda is always cheaper than EC2 (depends on usage pattern)
Lambda scales infinitely (concurrency limits apply)

name: ECS and Fargate
description: Container orchestration with optional serverless compute
key_facts: ECS: AWS-native container orchestration
Fargate: serverless compute for containers (no EC2 management)
EC2 launch type: more control, GPUs, spot instances
Task definitions define container configurations
Service auto-scaling based on CloudWatch metrics
common_misconceptions: Fargate supports GPUs (it does not, use EC2 launch type)
ECS is the same as EKS (ECS is AWS-native, EKS is Kubernetes)

name: S3 Storage Classes
description: S3 tiered storage for cost optimization
key_facts: Standard: frequent access, 99.99% availability
Standard-IA: infrequent, min 30-day storage charge
One Zone-IA: single AZ, 20% cheaper than Standard-IA
Glacier Instant: millisecond retrieval
Glacier Flexible: minutes to hours retrieval
Glacier Deep Archive: 12-48 hour retrieval, cheapest
Intelligent-Tiering: automatic tier movement
common_misconceptions: Glacier retrieval is always slow (Instant Retrieval is milliseconds)
Intelligent-Tiering has retrieval fees (it doesn't for frequent/infrequent)

name: S3 Lifecycle Policies
description: Automated object transition and expiration rules
key_facts: Transition actions move objects between storage classes
Expiration actions delete objects after specified time
Can filter by prefix or tags
Minimum 30 days before transitioning from Standard
Cannot transition from Glacier back to Standard automatically
common_misconceptions: Lifecycle rules can move objects back to Standard (they can't)
Transitions happen instantly (they're processed asynchronously)

name: RDS and Aurora
description: Managed relational databases with Aurora's cloud-native architecture
key_facts: Aurora: 5x throughput of MySQL, 3x of PostgreSQL
Aurora storage auto-scales up to 128 TB
Aurora replicas: up to 15, with auto-failover
Aurora Global Database: cross-region with <1 second replication
RDS Multi-AZ: synchronous standby for HA
RDS Read Replicas: async, can be cross-region
common_misconceptions: RDS Multi-AZ improves read performance (it's for HA only)
Aurora Serverless v2 has no minimum capacity (it has 0.5 ACU minimum)

name: DynamoDB
description: Serverless NoSQL database with single-digit millisecond performance
key_facts: Key-value and document data model
Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale
On-demand or provisioned capacity modes
DynamoDB Streams for change data capture
Global Tables for multi-region replication
DAX for microsecond read caching
common_misconceptions: DynamoDB is good for complex joins (it's not, use RDS/Aurora)
On-demand mode is always cheaper (provisioned can be cheaper for predictable workloads)

name: 7 Rs Migration Strategies
description: Seven migration strategies from rehost to retire
key_facts: Rehost (lift and shift): move as-is to EC2
Replatform: minor optimizations (e.g., RDS instead of self-managed DB)
Repurchase: move to SaaS (e.g., CRM to Salesforce)
Refactor/Re-architect: redesign for cloud-native
Retire: decommission applications no longer needed
Retain: keep on-premises for now
Relocate: move to VMware Cloud on AWS
common_misconceptions: Refactoring is always the best strategy (it's the most expensive and time-consuming)
Rehosting provides no cloud benefits (it does: HA, scalability, pay-as-you-go)

name: Database Migration Service
description: Managed database migration with minimal downtime
key_facts: Supports homogeneous and heterogeneous migrations
Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for heterogeneous migrations
Continuous data replication (CDC) for minimal downtime
Supports migration to RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, S3
Replication instance runs in a VPC
common_misconceptions: DMS handles schema conversion (SCT does that separately)
DMS is only for relational databases (it supports DynamoDB and S3 too)

name: Cost Optimization Strategies
description: EC2 pricing models and cost management tools
key_facts: Reserved Instances: 1 or 3 year commitment, up to 72% savings
Savings Plans: flexible commitment, up to 72% savings
Spot Instances: up to 90% savings, can be interrupted
Compute Savings Plans: flexible across instance families
Cost Explorer: analyze spending patterns
Budgets: set custom cost alerts
common_misconceptions: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans are the same (Savings Plans are more flexible)
Spot Instances are unreliable for all workloads (they're fine for fault-tolerant batch jobs)
